work.title: Software Developer Co-op
work.description:
During my Software Developer Co-op at MANTECH, I worked on developing and improving Windows desktop software used with automated laboratory instrumentation. My work involved building production-level features in C# and .NET, contributing to both frontend and backend development, debugging issues, reviewing existing code, and learning how large software systems are designed and maintained in a professional environment.

MANTECH is a Guelph-based company that manufactures laboratory, process, and portable analyzers for water, soil, and food testing. Its systems are used in settings such as laboratories, universities, factories, and field operations, supporting faster and more sustainable analysis. This made the work especially meaningful because the software I contributed to was connected to real scientific and environmental applications.

One of my main learning goals was to improve my problem-solving and software design skills by learning how to build loosely coupled and maintainable software. Through this term, I gained a stronger understanding of dependency injection, design patterns, and how different components in a codebase should interact. At first, I focused mainly on making features work, but over time I started thinking more carefully about long-term maintainability, testability, and how future developers would use or extend my code. Code reviews were especially helpful because they showed me how experienced developers evaluate design decisions, not just whether the code runs.

Another important goal was to strengthen my technical literacy in C# and Windows desktop application development. By working on real production features, bug fixes, and refactoring tasks, I became much more comfortable navigating a large codebase and understanding how UI, business logic, data access, and hardware-related workflows fit together. Many of the skills I used were introduced in my computer science courses, such as object-oriented programming, data structures, debugging, and software design. However, applying those concepts in a real-world codebase taught me how much professional software development depends on readability, collaboration, testing, and making careful changes to existing systems.

My third goal was to improve my written communication. I practiced this through pull request descriptions, documentation, code comments, and written updates to my team. This helped me become more intentional about explaining my work clearly and concisely. I learned that good communication is part of good engineering because other developers need to understand not only what changed, but why it changed. Over the term, I became more comfortable discussing my work, asking for feedback, and explaining technical ideas in a way that supported team collaboration.

Overall, this work term helped me grow from someone who could complete programming tasks into someone who thinks more like a software developer working on a shared, long-term product. I improved my C# and desktop development skills, gained practical experience with software architecture and dependency injection, and developed stronger communication habits. This experience gave me a better understanding of how software supports real-world scientific instruments and prepared me to take on more complex development work in future co-op terms and professional roles.

work.title: Undergraduate Research Assistant
work.description: Built an interactive assessment tool using JavaScript, enabling researchers to analyze social isolation metrics.

work.title: Software Engineer Co-op
work.description: During my Software Engineer Co-op at CIBC, I supported the QA team in writing and maintaining functional test cases for middleware and infrastructure tools. I learned the structure and standards for creating test cases, contributed several that were validated and used by the team, and participated in test reviews to improve quality and coverage. This strengthened my critical thinking and problem-solving skills by anticipating edge cases and identifying potential failures. While I was not directly assigned coding work, I gained exposure to automation scripts and internal workflows, participated in peer reviews, and contributed ideas for potential improvements. I also enhanced my oral communication skills by providing updates in team stand-ups, documenting incidents, and clearly sharing progress with both technical and non-technical stakeholders. Feedback from team leads confirmed that my communication was effective and valuable for team alignment.
work.description2: CIBC (Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce) is one of Canada’s largest banks, headquartered in Toronto, serving clients globally with a strong focus on financial services and technology innovation. This co-op term gave me first-hand experience in quality assurance and infrastructure support in a professional banking environment. Through my goals, I developed problem-solving abilities, deepened my understanding of technological literacy, and strengthened oral communication skills. Although I was not able to fully achieve my scripting goal, exposure to automation practices broadened my knowledge of industry standards. Overall, this work term reinforced the importance of collaboration, critical thinking, and adaptability, preparing me for future co-op opportunities and professional roles in software engineering.

work.title: Software Developer Intern
work.description: Developed a prototype neural network using TensorFlow for image classification to detect diseases in cow lymph nodes, trained on a dataset of over 3,500 annotated DICOM-formatted X-ray images.
work.description2: Designed and implemented a Python-based image format converter program, streamlining the preprocessing of DICOM images for training the neural network.
